name: latexcodec

metadata.name: latexcodec
metadata.summary: A lexer and codec to work with LaTeX code in Python.

documentation:
---
substitutions:
  ci: |-
    ```{image} https://github.com/mcmtroffaes/latexcodec/actions/workflows/python-package.yml/badge.svg
    :alt: ci
    :target: https://github.com/mcmtroffaes/latexcodec/actions/workflows/python-package.yml
    ```
  codecov: |-
    ```{image} https://codecov.io/gh/mcmtroffaes/latexcodec/branch/develop/graph/badge.svg
    :alt: codecov
    ```

---

- **Instead of using latexcodec, I encourage you to consider pylatexenc instead, which is far superior:** <https://github.com/phfaist/pylatexenc>
- Download: <http://pypi.python.org/pypi/latexcodec/#downloads>
- Documentation: <http://latexcodec.readthedocs.org/>
- Development: <http://github.com/mcmtroffaes/latexcodec/>

The codec provides a convenient way of going between text written in
LaTeX and unicode. Since it is not a LaTeX compiler, it is more
appropriate for short chunks of text, such as a paragraph or the
values of a BibTeX entry, and it is not appropriate for a full LaTeX
document. In particular, its behavior on the LaTeX commands that do
not simply select characters is intended to allow the unicode
representation to be understandable by a human reader, but is not
canonical and may require hand tuning to produce the desired effect.

The encoder does a best effort to replace unicode characters outside
of the range used as LaTeX input (ascii by default) with a LaTeX
command that selects the character. More technically, the unicode code
point is replaced by a LaTeX command that selects a glyph that
reasonably represents the code point. Unicode characters with special
uses in LaTeX are replaced by their LaTeX equivalents. For example,

| original text        | encoded LaTeX     |
| -------------------- | ----------------- |
| `¥`                  | `\yen`            |
| `ü`                  | `\"u`             |
| `\N{NO-BREAK SPACE}` | `~`               |
| `~`                  | `\textasciitilde` |
| `%`                  | `\%`              |
| `#`                  | `\#`              |
| `\textbf{x}`         | `\textbf{x}`      |

The decoder does a best effort to replace LaTeX commands that select
characters with the unicode for the character they are selecting. For
example,

| original LaTeX    | decoded unicode      |
| ----------------- | -------------------- |
| `\yen`            | `¥`                  |
| `\"u`             | `ü`                  |
| `~`               | `\N{NO-BREAK SPACE}` |
| `\textasciitilde` | `~`                  |
| `\%`              | `%`                  |
| `\#`              | `#`                  |
| `\textbf{x}`      | `\textbf {x}`        |
| `#`               | `#`                  |

In addition, comments are dropped (including the final newline that
marks the end of a comment), paragraphs are canonicalized into double
newlines, and other newlines are left as is. Spacing after LaTeX
commands is also canonicalized.

For example,

```
hi % bye
there\par world
\textbf     {awesome}
```

is decoded as

```
hi there

world
\textbf {awesome}
```

When decoding, LaTeX commands not directly selecting characters (for
example, macros and formatting commands) are passed through
unchanged. The same happens for LaTeX commands that select characters
but are not yet recognized by the codec.  Either case can result in a
hybrid unicode string in which some characters are understood as
literally the character and others as parts of unexpanded commands.
Consequently, at times, backslashes will be left intact for denoting
the start of a potentially unrecognized control sequence.

Given the numerous and changing packages providing such LaTeX
commands, the codec will never be complete, and new translations of
unrecognized unicode or unrecognized LaTeX symbols are always welcome.

modules.name: latexcodec.codec
documentation:
# LaTeX Codec

The {mod}`latexcodec.codec` module
contains all classes and functions for LaTeX code
translation. For practical use,
you should only ever need to import the {mod}`latexcodec` module,
which will automatically register the codec
so it can be used by {meth}`str.encode`, {meth}`str.decode`,
and any of the functions defined in the {mod}`codecs` module
such as {func}`codecs.open` and so on.
The other functions and classes
are exposed in case someone would want to extend them.

```
.. autofunction:: register
```

```
.. autofunction:: find_latex
```

```
.. autoclass:: LatexIncrementalEncoder
    :show-inheritance:
    :members:
```

```
.. autoclass:: LatexIncrementalDecoder
    :show-inheritance:
    :members:
```

```
.. autoclass:: LatexCodec
    :show-inheritance:
    :members:
```

```
.. autoclass:: LatexUnicodeTable
    :members:
```

functions.name: latexcodec.codec.register
documentation:
Register the {func}`find_latex` codec search function.

:::{seealso}
{func}`codecs.register`
:::

functions.name: latexcodec.codec.getregentry
documentation: Encodings module API.
returns the codec search function
this is used if latex_codec.py were to be placed in stdlib

functions.name: latexcodec.codec.find_latex
documentation:
Return a {class}`codecs.CodecInfo` instance for the requested
LaTeX *encoding*, which must be equal to `latex`,
or to `latex+<encoding>`
where `<encoding>` describes another encoding.

modules.name: latexcodec.lexer
documentation:
# LaTeX Lexer

This module contains all classes for lexing LaTeX code, as well as
general purpose base classes for incremental LaTeX decoders and
encoders, which could be useful in case you are writing your own
custom LaTeX codec.

```
.. autoclass:: Token(name, text)
```

```
.. autoclass:: LatexLexer
   :show-inheritance:
   :members:
```

```
.. autoclass:: LatexIncrementalLexer
   :show-inheritance:
   :members:
```

```
.. autoclass:: LatexIncrementalDecoder
   :show-inheritance:
   :members:
```

```
.. autoclass:: LatexIncrementalEncoder
   :show-inheritance:
   :members:
```

classes.name: latexcodec.lexer.MetaRegexpLexer
documentation:
Metaclass for {class}`RegexpLexer`. Compiles tokens into a
regular expression.
implementation note: we derive from IncrementalDecoder because this
class serves excellently as a base class for incremental decoders,
but of course we don't decode yet until later

classes.name: latexcodec.lexer.RegexpLexer

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.RegexpLexer.__init__
documentation: Initialize the codec.

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.RegexpLexer.reset
documentation: Reset state.

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.RegexpLexer.getstate
documentation: Get state.

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.RegexpLexer.setstate
documentation:
Set state. The *state* must correspond to the return value
of a previous {meth}`getstate` call.

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.RegexpLexer.get_raw_tokens
documentation:
Yield tokens without any further processing. Tokens are one of:

- `\<word>`: a control word (i.e. a command)
- `\<symbol>`: a control symbol (i.e. ^ etc.)
- `#<n>`: a parameter
- a series of byte characters

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.RegexpLexer.flush_raw_tokens
documentation: Flush the raw token buffer.

documentation: : The empty token.

documentation: : Sequence of token regexps.

class_variables.name: latexcodec.lexer.RegexpLexer.errors
documentation: : How to respond to errors.

class_variables.name: latexcodec.lexer.RegexpLexer.raw_buffer
documentation: : The raw buffer of this lexer.

class_variables.name: latexcodec.lexer.RegexpLexer.regexp
documentation: : Compiled regular expression.
Abstract base class for regexp based lexers.

classes.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexLexer

class_variables.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexLexer.tokens
documentation: List of token names, and the regular expressions they match.
A very simple lexer for tex/latex.

classes.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalLexer

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalLexer.get_tokens
documentation:
Yield tokens while maintaining a state. Also skip
whitespace after control words and (some) control symbols.
Replaces newlines by spaces and \par commands depending on
the context.

A very simple incremental lexer for tex/latex code. Roughly
follows the state machine described in Tex By Topic, Chapter 2.

The generated tokens satisfy:

* no newline characters: paragraphs are separated by '\par'
* spaces following control tokens are compressed

classes.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalDecoder

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalDecoder.decode_token
documentation:
Returns the decoded token text.

:::{note}
Control words get an extra space added at the back to make
sure separation from the next token, so that decoded token
sequences can be joined together.

For example, the tokens `'\hello'` and `'world'`
will correctly result in `'\hello world'` (remember
that LaTeX eats space following control words). If no space
were added, this would wrongfully result in
`'\helloworld'`.
:::

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalDecoder.get_unicode_tokens
documentation:
Decode every token. Override to
process the tokens in some other way (for example, for token
translation).

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalDecoder.udecode
documentation:
Decode LaTeX *bytes\_* into a unicode string.

This implementation calls {meth}`get_unicode_tokens` and joins
the resulting unicode strings together.

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalDecoder.decode
documentation:
Decode LaTeX *bytes\_* into a unicode string. Implementation uses
{meth}`udecode`.

class_variables.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalDecoder.inputenc
documentation: Input encoding. **Must** extend ascii.

Simple incremental decoder. Transforms lexed LaTeX tokens into
unicode.

To customize decoding, subclass and override
{meth}`get_unicode_tokens`.

classes.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalEncoder

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalEncoder.__init__
documentation: Initialize the codec.

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalEncoder.reset
documentation: Reset state.

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalEncoder.getstate
documentation: Get state.

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalEncoder.setstate
documentation:
Set state. The *state* must correspond to the return value
of a previous {meth}`getstate` call.

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalEncoder.get_unicode_tokens
documentation:
Split unicode into tokens so that every token starts with a
non-combining character.

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalEncoder.flush_unicode_tokens
documentation: Flush the buffer.

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalEncoder.get_latex_chars
documentation:
Encode every character. Override to
process the unicode in some other way (for example, for character
translation).

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalEncoder.uencode
documentation:
Encode the *unicode\_* string into LaTeX {class}`bytes`.

This implementation calls {meth}`get_latex_chars` and joins
the resulting {class}`bytes` together.

methods.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalEncoder.encode
documentation:
Encode the *unicode\_* string into LaTeX {class}`bytes`.

This implementation calls {meth}`get_latex_chars` and joins
the resulting {class}`bytes` together.

class_variables.name: latexcodec.lexer.LatexIncrementalEncoder.inputenc
documentation: Input encoding. **Must** extend ascii.

Simple incremental encoder for LaTeX. Transforms unicode into
{class}`bytes`.

To customize decoding, subclass and override
{meth}`get_latex_bytes`.
